summary: Gruber revisits a notorious 2001 Businessweek article predicting Apple's retail stores would fail, arguing it revealed a deeper misunderstanding about Apple that persists today. The original critique framed Apple as a caviar seller in a cheese-and-crackers world, implying superior design and mass market appeal are mutually exclusive. Gruber contends this gets Apple fundamentally wrong: the company is a luxury brand for the masses, selling better-tasting cheese and crackers, not exclusive caviar. He argues Apple's retail stores — welcoming, crowded, casual — embody this inclusiveness and let customers experience product quality firsthand. The critics who predict Apple's downfall are actually the elitists, assuming mass-market consumers are too tasteless to appreciate quality. Apple's scale and design excellence reinforce each other rather than working at cross purposes.
key_points: The 2001 prediction that Apple Stores would fail wasn't a fringe opinion — it reflected widespread consensus among investors and tech press, rooted in a fundamental misunderstanding of Apple's relationship with customers
The caviar-versus-cheese-and-crackers framing falsely assumes superior design and mass market appeal are mutually exclusive, when Apple has proven they reinforce each other through economies of scale
Apple's critics are the real elitists — they assume mass-market consumers are tasteless and fickle, unable to discern or care about product quality
Apple's retail stores succeed precisely because they're inclusive and welcoming rather than stuffy and exclusive, letting anyone experience product quality firsthand
The pattern of predicting Apple's imminent commoditization by Android mirrors the same flawed thinking from a decade earlier
money_quotes: The part that's wrong is the insistence that broad mass market appeal and an insistence upon superior design are mutually exclusive.
Apple's brand stands for both quality and inclusiveness. It's a luxury brand for the masses.

They accuse Apple of pretension and elitism, but it's they, the critics, who hold that the mass market for phones and tablets is overwhelmingly composed of tasteless, fickle shoppers who neither discern nor care about product quality.
Apple is not selling caviar against cheese and crackers. They're selling better-tasting cheese and crackers, and all you have to do is come into their store and taste some to believe for yourself.
Advertising alone can't convince customers that products are nice, because all ads claim every product is great. You need to see things, to touch and try them, to truly believe.
By selling very large quantities of a remarkably small number of products, Apple operates with economies of scale that are the envy of all its competitors.
His case was based on a severe misunderstanding of Apple as a company, of its relationship with its customers, and of its then-potential for the coming decade.
key_insight: Apple's critics fundamentally misunderstand the company by assuming superior design and mass market appeal are mutually exclusive, when in reality many people from all walks of life simply appreciate nice things.
